reasoning:
May 18 (Date 1): The May 18 resolution relies on the Wednesday, May 14 data. Federal Reserve maturities predictably occur mid-month (15th) and end-of-month. Thus, the May 14 report excludes May's primary roll-offs. In standard second-week intervals (e.g., Feb 12, Mar 12, Apr 9), the absence of maturing securities combined with daily TIPS inflation compensation predictably causes a slight week-over-week increase in securities held outright. Therefore, a slight increase from May 7 to May 14 is highly probable.

June 10 (Date 2): Resolves using June 4 data. By this point, both the May 15 and May 31 expected quantitative tightening (QT) runoffs will have settled. Despite the Fed's recent reduction of the Treasury runoff cap to $5B/month (down from $25B), combined with ~$10-15B in organic MBS runoff, the total May draw-down will be roughly $15-20B, ensuring the June figure drops below the early May baseline.

August 9 (Date 3) & November 7 (Date 4): While QT runoffs during June and July will compound the decline through August, market consensus heavily expects QT to end completely by roughly Q3 2025 (June–July). Following the conclusion of QT, the Fed's framework indicates they intend to stabilize reserves and gradually resume organic securities purchases to track with currency-in-circulation demands. Because the pace of summer QT runoff has sharply decelerated, a subsequent few months of organic balance sheet growth by November could feasibly overcome the summer slump, restoring the total securities to above the early-May level. Consequently, the conditional probability of exceeding the initial baseline rebounds significantly as the horizon extends to November, perfectly satisfying the requirement of broadly increasing uncertainty.
